figures.caption: Figure 1: Qualitative IntrA comparisons on representative meshes, with the top half in the canonical orientation and the bottom half after a 40° z-axis rotation. Under rotation, DGCNN and PTv3 show visibly noisier aneurysm boundaries and more leakage into the parent vessel, whereas VN+EAMS remains visually consistent across both conditions. Appendix Figure 6 adds the omitted EAMS and SRA+EAMS predictions for the same cases.

Table 14: Full robustness results for liver surface segmentation on the dataset of [11]. Results are mean ± std over five cross-validation folds. CD is reported after multiplying by 100. Table 3 shows the corresponding Dice and CD summaries for the baseline and 40° conditions.

Table 2: Intraoral scan tooth segmentation on 3D-IOSSeg [9] and Teeth3DS [8]. Metric is Average IoU (%), obtained by averaging each mesh over its ground-truth-present classes and then averaging over meshes, not by averaging the final per-class IoU summaries. 3D-IOSSeg results are mean ± std over five cross-validation folds; Teeth3DS results are from a single fold (no std). Bold: best per column (all rows); underline: second-best. Due to PCA sign-resolution heuristics in the derived frames, the EAMS variants are only approximately SE(3)-invariant on these datasets, so we report empirical robustness under all perturbations. Per-class results are in Tables 10 and 11.

Figure 2: Qualitative tooth-segmentation comparisons, with the left half showing the canonical orientation and the right half showing a 40° z-axis rotation. Fast-TGCN is competitive at baseline but visibly degrades near posterior teeth and gingival boundaries under rotation, whereas VN+EAMS remains stable across both settings.
Table 7: Per-dataset default feature configurations and input dimensionalities. The coordinate channels column counts the number of R3 vector channels propagated alongside the scalar node features.

Figure 6: Additional qualitative IntrA comparison for the invariant mesh variants on the same cases used in Figure 1, with the top half showing the canonical orientation and the bottom half showing a 40° z-axis rotation. This appendix-only figure restores the omitted EAMS and SRA+EAMS predictions, showing that all three mesh-based variants remain visually stable under 40° rotation, with SRA+EAMS and VN+EAMS typically producing the cleanest aneurysm boundaries.

Table 3: Liver surface segmentation on the dataset of [11]. Results are mean ± std over five crossvalidation folds. CD is reported after multiplying by 100. Bold: best per column (all rows); underline: second-best. †: MeshGraphCNN results are our own reproduction. Full IoU, CD×100, and HD results are in Table 14.

Figure 3: Qualitative liver-surface comparisons on two representative meshes, with the top half in the canonical orientation and the bottom half after a 40° z-axis rotation. MeshGraphCNN uses a slightly different liver mesh because of its preprocessing requirements (Appendix A). All three baselines degrade substantially under rotation, especially on the thin ligament regions. Appendix Figure 7 qualitatively compares three EAMS-family columns.

Table 10: Per-class IoU (%) for the left jaw half on 3D-IOSSeg [9] (Gingiva and T11/31–T18/38). Results are mean ± std over five cross-validation folds. Baseline scores are mostly unchanged under rotation.

Figure 7: Additional qualitative liver comparison for the EAMS-family methods on two representative cases from Figure 3, with the top half in the canonical orientation and the bottom half after a 40° z-axis rotation.

Table 9: Full robustness results for intracranial aneurysm segmentation on the IntrA dataset. Results are mean ± std over five cross-validation folds. EAMS, SRA+EAMS, and VN+EAMS are E(3)invariant; their scores are identical to Table 1 under all listed transformations and are omitted.
Figure 4: Representative ground-truth segmentations from the three benchmark datasets used in our evaluation.
Table 4: Single-fold IntrA ablations using IoU only. PA = parent vessel, AN = aneurysm.
Table 11: Per-class IoU (%) for the right jaw half on 3D-IOSSeg [9] (T21/41–T28/48). Results are mean ± std over five cross-validation folds. Baseline scores are mostly unchanged under rotation.

Table 13: Per-class IoU (%) for the right jaw half on Teeth3DS [8] (T21/41–T28/48). Single-fold results; no standard deviation reported. All conditions listed for all methods due to PCA-induced imperfect SE(3)-invariance.

Table 12: Per-class IoU (%) for the left jaw half on Teeth3DS [8] (Gingiva and T11/31–T18/38). Single-fold results; no standard deviation reported. All conditions listed for all methods due to PCA-induced imperfect SE(3)-invariance.
